Section 01

The Essential Sites

Everything walkable from the Plaza Hotel. Book Acropolis tickets before you fly.

Ancient Monument

The Acropolis & Parthenon

Ακρόπολη & Παρθενώνας

The single most important ancient site in the Western world. The limestone hill with the Parthenon on top is 2,500 years old and still enormous. You'll see the Erechtheion with replicas of the Caryatids statues, the Temple of Athena Nike, and the Propylaea (the monumental entrance). Go early morning to beat both the heat and the crowds. The Parthenon's scaffolding has been removed and it's fully revealed for the first time in decades.

Cost €30 per adult (flat rate, year-round). Timed entry required.
Hours 8:00 AM — 8:00 PM (summer). Last entry 30 min before close.
Book Tickets hhticket.gr — official government site. Book 5-7 days before.
Time Needed 2 — 3 hours
Open in Maps
Free Experience · Sunset

Areopagus Hill (Mars Hill)

Άρειος Πάγος

The single best free experience in Athens. A large smooth rock outcrop just below the Acropolis with a panoramic view of the entire city. At sunset, locals and tourists sit side by side with wine and snacks watching the sky change colour. The walk takes five minutes from the Acropolis entrance on Theorias Street. Be careful: the rock has been polished smooth by millions of feet and can be slippery.

Cost FREE
Best Time Sunset. Arrive 30 minutes early for a good spot.
Access Entrance on Theorias Street, right by the main Acropolis entrance.
Bring Wine, water, snacks. Wear shoes with grip.
Open in Maps
Ancient Site · Museum

Ancient Agora & Temple of Hephaestus

Αρχαία Αγορά & Ναός Ηφαίστου

The marketplace and political heart of ancient Athens, where everything happened: shopping, socialising, philosophy, politics. The Temple of Hephaestus inside the complex is the best-preserved ancient temple in all of Greece. The excellent museum is housed inside the reconstructed Stoa of Attalos, a two-storey colonnade that gives you a visceral sense of scale. This was where Socrates walked and argued. Allow at least two hours.

Cost €10 (separate ticket required since combo discontinued)
Location Adrianou 24, Athens 105 55. Short walk from Monastiraki.
Time Needed 2 — 2.5 hours
Tip Combine with the Monastiraki Flea Market afterwards.
Open in Maps
Ancient Monument

Temple of Olympian Zeus & Hadrian's Gate

Ναός Ολυμπίου Διός & Πύλη Αδριανού

Once one of the largest temples in the ancient world with 104 massive columns. Only 15 remain, but they are undeniably impressive. Construction started in the 6th century BC and took over 600 years to complete, finished under the Roman Emperor Hadrian in 131 AD. Hadrian's Gate, a massive arch nearby, was built in 131-132 AD to honour him. The gate is free to view and photograph. Note: the temple currently has restoration scaffolding around the columns.

Cost €8 (separate ticket). Hadrian's Gate is free.
Location Vasilissis Olgas, Athens 105 57. Short walk from Acropolis metro.
Time Needed 45 minutes — 1 hour
Open in Maps
World-Class Museum

National Archaeological Museum

Εθνικό Αρχαιολογικό Μουσείο

If you visit one museum in Athens, make it this one. The single most important collection of Greek antiquities in the world, with artefacts dating back to 5000 BC. You'll see the golden Mask of Agamemnon, the statue of Zeus/Poseidon, the Antikythera Mechanism (the world's oldest analogue computer), and an impressive Egyptian collection. The museum is well laid out but enormous; plan which exhibits you want to prioritise. The on-site cafe is a welcome rest stop.

Cost €12 (standard adult)
Location 28is Oktovriou 44, Athens 106 82
Getting There Metro to Victoria station (Line 1). Or taxi/Uber, ~€5-7 from Plaka.
Time Needed 3 — 4 hours for a thorough visit
Open in Maps
Modern Museum

Acropolis Museum

Μουσείο Ακρόπολης

A stunning modern building sitting below the Acropolis hill, opened in 2009. The glass floor on the ground level reveals active archaeological excavations beneath the building. The top floor is oriented to mirror the Parthenon above, with the remaining original frieze panels displayed in their correct positions. Beautifully curated, well lit, and air-conditioned. A perfect midday escape from the heat.

Cost €20 (standard). Book at etickets.theacropolismuseum.gr
Location Dionysiou Areopagitou 15, Athens 117 42
Getting There 5-minute walk from Akropoli metro station.
Time Needed 2 — 3 hours
Open in Maps
Panoramic Views · Free / Low Cost

Lycabettus Hill

Λυκαβηττός

The highest peak in Athens. The view stretches across the entire city, the Acropolis, the Aegean Sea, and on a clear day as far as the nearby islands. There's a small white chapel (Agios Georgios) at the summit and a cafe/restaurant. Sunset is the most magical time. This is also an excellent "could we live here?" vantage point: you can see the entire shape of Athens and understand how the neighbourhoods fit together.

Cost FREE to walk up. Cable car €10 return.
Getting There Metro to Syntagma or Panepistimio. 20-min hike from the base.
Best Time Late afternoon for golden light, or sunset.
Tip Take the cable car up, walk down. Bring water.
Open in Maps
Ancient Monument · Olympic History

Panathenaic Stadium

Παναθηναϊκό Στάδιο (Καλλιμάρμαρο)

The only stadium in the world built entirely of marble. Capacity of 60,000. Originally constructed in 330 BC, it was rebuilt for the first modern Olympic Games in 1896. You can walk on the track, sit in the marble seats, and visit the small Olympic museum in the tunnel beneath the stands. The sheer scale of it is difficult to grasp from photographs. An audio guide is included with admission.

Cost €10 (includes audio guide)
Location Leof. Vasileos Konstantinou, Athens 116 35
Time Needed 1 — 1.5 hours
Getting There 15-minute walk from Syntagma Square through the National Garden.
Open in Maps
Neighbourhood · Free to Wander

Monastiraki Square & Psyrri Quarter

Μοναστηράκι & Ψυρρή

Monastiraki is the beating heart of Athens. The square has a direct view of the Acropolis, and on Sundays the area explodes into a huge flea market with vintage vinyl, handmade jewellery, and antique coins. Psyrri, just behind Monastiraki, was once a grim industrial area and is now one of Athens' most exciting neighbourhoods. Entire buildings are covered in street art murals. In the evening the square buzzes with street performers and live music. This is the area where you'll feel the city's energy most intensely.

Cost FREE
Flea Market Every day, but Sunday is the big one. Starts early morning.
Getting There Monastiraki metro station drops you right on the square.
Open in Maps
Evening Experience

Thiseio Open Air Cinema

Θησείον — Σινεμά

One of the oldest open-air cinemas in Athens, tucked in a quiet spot near the Acropolis. Films are screened in their original language with Greek subtitles. The Acropolis, lit up behind the screen, is the backdrop. A mix of international classics and newer releases. No advance booking needed: just show up, buy your ticket at the counter, and grab popcorn or a cold beer from the kiosk. Arrive a bit early for a good seat. A proper Athenian experience on a warm evening.

Cost ~€8-9 per ticket
Location Apostolou Pavlou 7, Thissio, Athens 118 51
Season Open from late May. Check showings locally.
Open in Maps
Section 02

Walking Routes

All routes start from or near the Plaza Hotel in central Athens. Beautiful historical walks you can do at your own pace.

Route 01

The Grand Archaeological Walk

⏱ 4-5 hours 📍 ~4 km 💰 €30-50 in tickets
1
Syntagma Square — Changing of the Guard
Start your morning here. Watch the evzonoi (presidential guards) in their traditional uniforms at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. Full ceremony on Sundays at 11:00 AM; smaller hourly changes every day. Free. → Maps
2
National Garden
Walk south through the 16-hectare park behind Parliament. Gravel paths, ponds, shade. Free. A welcome cool corridor before the heat of the day. Exit on the south side toward the stadium. → Maps
3
Panathenaic Stadium
The all-marble Olympic stadium. €10. 1 hour. Then walk west along Vasilissis Olgas toward the Temple of Zeus. → Maps
4
Temple of Olympian Zeus & Hadrian's Gate
The 15 surviving columns and the arch. €8 for the temple; gate is free. 45 minutes. Then walk up toward the Acropolis. → Maps
5
Dionysiou Areopagitou Walkway
The grand pedestrian boulevard along the south side of the Acropolis. Wide, tree-lined, with the Acropolis looming above you. One of the most beautiful urban walks in Europe. Connects to the Acropolis Museum. Free. → Maps
6
The Acropolis
The main event. €30, pre-booked timed entry. 2-3 hours. Enter from the south slope entrance to avoid the longer queue at the main gate. → Maps
7
Areopagus Hill — Sunset
End the day here. Free. Bring wine and snacks from a kiosk. Watch the sun set over Athens with the Acropolis lit up behind you. → Maps
Route 02

Old Athens: Plaka & Anafiotika

⏱ 2-3 hours 📍 ~2.5 km 💰 Free (just food & coffee)
1
Ermou Street
Start at Syntagma and walk down the main pedestrian shopping avenue. You'll pass a ruined Byzantine church sitting right in the middle of the modern street. The juxtaposition is very Athens. → Maps
2
Plaka — Lower Streets
Turn south into the car-free lanes. Bougainvillea-draped alleys, neoclassical mansions, small shops. The oldest district of Athens. Wander without an agenda. → Maps
3
Anafiotika
Walk uphill through Plaka and you'll stumble into this hidden neighbourhood clinging to the Acropolis cliff. Whitewashed houses, narrow stairways, cats. It feels like a Cycladic island village dropped into the middle of a capital city. Built by workers from the island of Anafi who were brought to Athens in the 1840s and built houses exactly like the ones they'd left behind. → Maps
4
Mnisikleous Street Steps
The famous stepped street in Plaka with restaurants and cafes lining both sides. Sit, have a coffee, and watch the city go by. This is one of the most photographed spots in Athens. → Maps
5
Lunch at Saita or To Kafeneio
End with a keto-friendly meal. Grilled lamb chops, Greek salad, tzatziki. See the Keto Dining section for details.
Route 03

"Could We Live Here?" Neighbourhood Scout

⏱ Half day 📍 ~5 km 💰 Free (coffee + supermarket browse)
1
Koukaki
A beautiful residential neighbourhood right under the Acropolis. Lots of younger Athenians and expats. Walk the streets, check the cafes, look at the apartment buildings. This is where many people who actually live in Athens choose to be. → Maps
2
Petralona
Just west of Koukaki. More local, fewer tourists. Family-oriented, excellent small tavernas, genuine neighbourhood feel. Walk through and see if the vibe resonates. → Maps
3
Pangrati
East of the Panathenaic Stadium. A leafy, residential area with a strong neighbourhood identity. Good schools, parks, local markets. Worth seeing if you're thinking about family life. → Maps
4
Kolonaki
The richest district of Athens. Beautiful boutiques, high-end restaurants, designer shops. The base of Lycabettus Hill. A very different feel from Koukaki or Petralona. → Maps
5
Supermarket Check
Pop into a Sklavenitis or AB Vassilopoulos. Check the keto-friendly aisle situation: meat, cheese, olive oil, fresh veg, Greek yogurt. See what's available and what it costs. This is the data point that actually matters for the move.

Keto Cheat Sheet for Greek Restaurants

Print this. Screenshot this. Keep it on your phone when ordering.

Order This

  • Horiatiki (Greek salad) — no croutons, extra feta
  • Tzatziki — yogurt, cucumber, garlic, olive oil
  • Melitzanosalata — roasted aubergine dip
  • Tirosalata — feta and ricotta cheese dip
  • Feta fourno — baked feta with olive oil
  • Grilled lamb chops (paidakia)
  • Grilled chicken (kotopoulo)
  • Souvlaki — meat only, skip the pita
  • Grilled octopus (htapodi)
  • Grilled whole fish (priced by weight)
  • Halloumi — grilled
  • Olives — always keto
  • Greek coffee "sketo" (unsweetened)

Skip This

  • Bread basket (it will arrive uninvited)
  • Moussaka — contains bechamel and potato
  • Pastitsio — pasta bake
  • Gemista — rice-stuffed tomatoes
  • Gigantes — giant beans in tomato
  • Dolmades — vine leaves stuffed with rice
  • Chips/fries (patates)
  • Pita bread
  • Baklava, loukoumades, any desserts
  • Sweetened coffee (specify "sketo")
  • Beer (carbs) — prefer wine or spirits
  • Any battered/fried fish (ask for grilled)
Section 03

Keto-Friendly Dining

Traditional tavernas are your best friend. Skip the fancy tourist restaurants with six-language menus.

Saita
Plaka — Kydathineon Street
Excellent for Keto

A genuinely authentic taverna on the quieter end of Kydathineon Street, with tables overlooking a Byzantine church courtyard. One of the rare Plaka spots that locals actually go to.

Keto order: Grilled lamb chops, baked aubergine with feta, Greek salad. Skip the bakaliaros skordalia (battered cod).
💰 €15-25/person 📍 Kydathineon 21, Plaka
Maps
To Kafeneio
Upper Plaka
Excellent for Keto

A genuine neighbourhood taverna in the quiet upper part of Plaka. The walk uphill filters out most tourists. Homemade dishes that taste like someone's grandmother made them.

Keto order: Lamb with artichokes, regional cheeses, barrel wine. Skip the moussaka.
💰 €15-25/person 📍 Upper Plaka (uphill walk)
Maps
Psaras (Palia Taverna tou Psara)
Plaka — Since 1898
Great for Keto (Seafood)

Historic fish taverna, better value than most seafood in Plaka. Fish is bought daily from the local market. Neoclassical building with outdoor seating.

Keto order: Grilled fish (ask price by weight first), grilled octopus, seafood mezedes. Skip the lobster pasta.
💰 €20-35/person 📍 Erechtheos 16, Plaka
Maps
Barbounaki
Plaka — Near Metropolitan Cathedral
Great for Keto (Seafood)

Run by the chef behind the famous Papaioannou restaurant in Piraeus. Focuses on seafood mezedes (sharing plates) rather than large single dishes. Tables outside overlooking the cathedral square.

Keto order: Grilled octopus, steamed mussels, grilled halloumi, calamari (grilled not fried). The red mullet is their signature.
💰 €20-30/person 📍 Plateia Mitropoleos, Plaka
Maps
Ta Karamanlidika tou Fani
Psyrri — Charcuterie & Deli
Excellent for Keto

A restaurant, deli, and charcuterie shop in one. Famous for its cured meats, aged cheeses, and traditional small plates. Well-known among locals. The whole place is basically a keto charcuterie board come to life.

Keto order: Charcuterie and cheese selection, tzatziki, any grilled meats. Everything on the deli counter is fair game.
💰 €20-30/person 📍 Evripidou 52, Athens 105 54
Maps
Ergon Athens House
Monastiraki — Ermou Street
Good for Keto

Inside the sleek Ergon Athens House. Everything sourced from small Greek producers. Stone walls, olive-wood tables, warm copper lighting. More upmarket but still grounded. Also has a ground-floor market where you can buy Greek olive oil, cheeses, and cured meats to take home.

Keto order: Slow-cooked meats, grilled fish, regional cheeses with olive oil. Skip the bean stews and pies.
💰 €30-45/person 📍 Mitropoleos 23, Athens 105 57
Maps
Platanos Taverna
Plaka — Since 1932
Good for Keto

Named after the plane trees that shade the tables on a peaceful square. Open since 1932. Henry Miller and Patrick Leigh Fermor ate here. Chequered tablecloths, old photographs, shadow puppet figures on the walls. As old-school Athens as it gets.

Keto order: Lamb or beef dishes, Greek salad drowned in olive oil with crushed feta. Skip the gigantes and gemista (rice-stuffed).
💰 €15-25/person 📍 Diogenous 4, Plaka, Athens 105 56
Maps
Scholarchio
Plaka — Off the Tourist Track
Good for Keto

Tucked away from the main tourist flow. Classic Greek fare with grilled meats, stews, and simple quality ingredients. Warm, casual, down-to-earth. A favourite among locals who want real food without the tourist markup.

Keto order: Grilled meats, feta, olives. The meat dishes are the strength here.
💰 €12-20/person 📍 Tripodon 14, Plaka, Athens
Maps
Section 04

Areas to Avoid & Safety

Athens is safe overall. These are the specific spots and scams to be aware of.

Omonia Square & Surrounds

The main area to steer clear of, especially at night. Drug dealing, pickpockets, petty crime. The streets between Omonia and Keramikos are the worst. Fine to pass through by day on a busy street, but don't linger after dark.

The National Archaeological Museum is near here. Take a taxi or metro directly to Victoria station, don't walk through Omonia to get there.

Exarcheia

Known for political activism, street art, and an alternative/bohemian scene. Some people love it. But it has pockets of open drug use and occasional unrest. Skip it at night; a daytime walk is fine if you stay on main streets.

If you want edgy street art, Psyrri has it without the safety concerns.

Metaxourgio

Close to Omonia. Has interesting restaurants and cafes by day, but some streets have drug-related activity after dark. Not worth the risk when there are so many better options nearby.

Filopappou Hill at Night

A beautiful hilltop with Acropolis views during the day, but poorly lit and isolated after sunset. Higher crime rates reported at night. Go during daytime only.

Metro Pickpockets

Line 1 is particularly known for this. Don't stand by the doors; move to the middle of the car. Wear bags in front. The "clumsy shove" is the classic move: someone bumps you while a partner lifts your phone or wallet. Validate your ticket every time; fines are steep.

Use FreeNow (taxi app) for short trips instead of the metro if you're carrying valuables.

Taxi & Restaurant Scams

Some taxi drivers inflate prices or take long routes. Download the FreeNow app before you arrive for transparent pricing. At restaurants, check the bill carefully; some tourist-area places add items you didn't order. Bread and water that appear uninvited may carry a charge.

Airport to central Athens: fixed fare of €40 (day) / €55 (night). Any driver quoting more is trying it on.
Section 05

Practical Information

Getting Around

  • Walk everywhere you can. Central Athens is compact and the joy is in the walking.
  • Metro: clean, cheap (~€1.20 single), runs until midnight. Syntagma, Akropoli, and Monastiraki are your main stations.
  • FreeNow app: the local Uber equivalent. Download before you fly.
  • Uber: exists but is just Uber Taxi (licensed local taxis, same fares).
Airport flat rate: €40 daytime (5am-midnight), €55 at night. Non-negotiable by law.

Tickets to Book Now

  • Acropolis: hhticket.gr — €30/person, timed entry. Book 5-7 days ahead.
  • Acropolis Museum: etickets.theacropolismuseum.gr — €20/person.
  • Other sites (Agora, Temple of Zeus, etc.) can be bought on the day or a day before.
The old combo ticket covering 7 sites has been discontinued. You need separate tickets for each site now.

Weather & Heat

  • Late May/early June: expect 25-32°C. Warm but not the brutal July/August oven.
  • Plan outdoor walking for mornings (before 11am) and late afternoons (after 5pm).
  • Midday: museums, restaurants, or shade. The Acropolis has no shade.
  • Carry water everywhere. Wear a hat. Sunscreen.

Money & Costs

  • Currency: Euro (€). Cards accepted almost everywhere.
  • Budget meal (taverna): €12-25 per person.
  • Nice meal: €25-45 per person.
  • Coffee: €2-4.
  • Metro single: ~€1.20.
  • Taxi across central Athens: €5-10.
Athens is significantly cheaper than London, particularly for food and drink. You'll eat extremely well without breaking the bank.

Keto Survival Kit

  • Bring your own stevia/sweetener packets.
  • Learn "sketo" (unsweetened) for coffee orders.
  • Greek supermarkets (Sklavenitis, AB Vassilopoulos) have excellent meat, cheese, olive oil, and yogurt.
  • Greek yogurt + a handful of raspberries from a supermarket = perfect keto snack.
  • Hotel breakfast buffets: eggs, cheese, olives, cold meats. Skip the pastries and bread.

Key Greek Phrases

  • Kalimera (Καλημέρα) — Good morning
  • Kalispera (Καλησπέρα) — Good evening
  • Efcharisto (Ευχαριστώ) — Thank you
  • Parakalo (Παρακαλώ) — Please / You're welcome
  • Sketo (Σκέτο) — Unsweetened (for coffee!)
  • Ton logariasmo (Τον λογαριασμό) — The bill, please
  • Yamas! (Γεια μας!) — Cheers!
Athens 2026

Prepared for Glen & Heather — 26 May to 1 June 2026

Plaza Hotel, Central Athens
